An Echo from the Old Bark School

It was the first Monday after the holidays. The children had taken their seats in the Old Bark School, and the master called out the roll as usual:вБ†вАФ

вАЬArvie Aspinall.вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬвАКвАЩEs, sir.вАЭ

вАЬDavid Cooper.вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬYes, sir.вАЭ

вАЬJohn Heegard.вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬYezzer.вАЭ

вАЬJoseph Swallow.вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬYesser.вАЭ

вАЬJames Bullock.вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬPresent.вАЭ

вАЬFrederick Swallow.вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬYвАЩsir.вАЭ

вАЬJames Nowlett.вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ (Chorus of вАЬAbsent.вАЭ)

вАЬWilliam Atkins.вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ (Chorus of вАЬAbsent.вАЭ)

вАЬDaniel Lyons.вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬPerresent, sor-r-r.вАЭ

Dan was a young immigrant, just out from the sod, and rolled his rвАЩs like a cock-dove. His brogue was rich enough to make an Irishman laugh.

Bill was вАЬwagging it.вАЭ His own especial chum was of the opinion that Bill was sick. The masterвАЩs opinion did not coincide, so he penned a note to WilliamвАЩs parents, to be delivered by the model boy of the school.

вАЬBertha Lambert.вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬYes, вАЩair.вАЭ

вАЬMay Carey.вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬPesinвАЩ, sair.вАЭ

вАЬRose Cooper.вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬYes, sir.вАЭ

вАЬJanet Wild.вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬY-y-yes, s-sir.вАЭ

вАЬMary Wild.вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

A solemn hush fell upon the school, and presently Janet Wild threw her arms out on the desk before her, let her face fall on them, and sobbed heart-brokenly. The master saw his mistake too late; he gave his head a little half-affirmative, half-negative movement, in that pathetic old way of his; rested his head on one hand, gazed sadly at the name, and sighed.

But the galoot of the school spoilt the pathos of it all, for, during the awed silence which followed the calling of the girlвАЩs name, he suddenly brightened upвБ†вАФthe first time he was ever observed to do so during school hoursвБ†вАФand said, briskly and cheerfully вАЬDeadвБ†вАФsir!вАЭ

He hadnвАЩt been able to answer a question correctly for several days.

вАЬChildren,вАЭ said the master gravely and sadly, вАЬchildren, this is the first time I ever had to put D to the name of one of my scholars. Poor Mary! she was one of my first pupilsвБ†вАФcame the first morning the school was opened. Children, I want you to be a little quieter today during play-hour, out of respect for the name of your dead schoolmate whom it has pleased the Almighty to take in her youth.вАЭ

вАЬPlease, sir,вАЭ asked the galoot, evidently encouraged by his fancied success, вАЬplease, sir, what does D stand for?вАЭ

вАЬDamn you for a hass!вАЭ snarled Jim Bullock between his teeth, giving the galoot a vicious dig in the side with his elbow.
